sentences.text: All right.
Yes.
Yes.
My name is Leon Wise.
And I was born in 1933.
In August, 1933, in Paris.
In the-- the 25th of August, 1933.
And--
It was a mixed up thing, you see.
When my parents came from Poland in 1927-- migrated to France-- somehow, I don't know the reason why, but I took my mother's name.
And then--
That was Cybulska.
C-Y-B-U-L-S-K-A.
And at a later stage, I took my father's name, which was the proper thing to do.
I don't know the reason why, but when I got my birth certificate, the proper name was this-- Wajselbaum.
W-A-J-S-E-L-B-A-U-M.
That's-- and I carried that name-- actually, both names-- through, through the war.
I don't know the reason why.
I think that because my parents were married in Poland religiously, not, not in a civil-- but then I investigated on my birth certificate and it gave me the proper name of my father.
So I, I have no idea why it became [INAUDIBLE].
I used the name of my mother Cybulska, you see?
And you were born in--
In Paris.
Dixieme arrondissement, which is tenth arrondissement.
And--
[FRENCH] And we lived in the 19th arrondissement.
The 19th arrondissement, before the war, was like-- I wouldn't say a Jewish ghetto-- but it was-- most, most Jews lived around there in that part of Paris.
And--
Yes.
My father's name was Simon-- which, is in English, is Simon.
My mother's name was Edith, Edith.
And I know very little about-- because I left when we separated because of the war.
I was only barely six years old.
My father was in the-- he was in the leather type of business.
He used to make shoes.
He used to make bags.
And I think he was also doing things with my uncle, my mother's brother, who is today, incidentally, still alive in Paris.
He's 89 years old.
His name is Cybulski.
Maurice Cybulski.
And he has a son, my cousin.
His name was Henry, Henry.
And we see each other whenever we can.
We speak to them on the phone and so on.
And I'm very fortunate to have that, at least, left over.
He's still alive.
Very vaguely, I know that my father was-- well, of course, we were a very close family.

And I was his-- I wouldn't say favorite, but because I was the youngest one-- and if I didn't eat, I remember he used to say to me, if you eat your meals, then I'll give you 20 centime and you can go down the road and buy some halva.
Because I was mad on halva, even today.
[LAUGHS]
You know?
So being a Jewish quarter, we always found somebody who was making halva in their area.
In-- near-- it was Belleville.
Belleville is 19th arrondissement.
Mhm.
Yeah.
And your family?
Your brothers and sisters?
We were-- right up to present time, we were together.
My sister is six years older than I am.
Her name is Raymonde.
She, she lives in Switzerland.
She's married, and her name is Feuerwerker.
And she has two, two girls, Shulamit and Beatrice.
Beatrice lives in Geneva.
Shulamit lives in London, and she has three children.
And Beatrice has two children.
My brother lives in Switzerland, as well.
He has two, two boys.
Two boys and a girl.
His name is Henry, Henry.
And he's 19 months older than I am.
Yes.
We, we speak on the phone quite often, and I saw him recently, in January.
Yes.
I can't-- no.
Very little, actually.
Very little.
The only type of memories I've got is really when we left home to go to this children's home.
Just visiting, you know, parks in Paris.
You know, that type of things, but-- and going to the Cheder, as well.
We used to go to the Cheder.
It was run by Rabbi Zalman Schneersohn, the original Schneersohn.
Incidentally, the one who passed away in New York last year was his cousin.
He was related to him.
But Rabbi Schneersohn had a lot of impact with us once we are-- you know, till, till the end of the war.
Yes.
Well, we used to have lots of fun.
It used to be close to the river Seine.
It was in rue de Dieu.
And I remember that he used to also run a kosher restaurant.
So my parents used to come every now and again when they-- you know, when my sister and my brother-- we used to go there together.
And I can remember one-- in 1939, the river was frozen.
The only time that the river Seine happens to be frozen.
And my sister-- we went across-- we walked across.
And it was this-- vaguely remember that.
You know, that was fun.
That's about all.
No, I can't remember that.
No.
No.
It's--
We were good boys, you see?
[LAUGHS] Yes.
Even friends-- family friends, yes.
I had cousins.
Also very vaguely that I can remember that.
But very little of it.
What sort of memories do you have of them?
Any impressions?
That-- not much impression, but we were together in-- going to the Cheder, visiting one another.
Sliding down-- what do you call-- stair case.
Because in Paris, there's a lot of-- up-- little, little roads, little streets with stair cases.
So we used to slide down the banister.
That type of things.
It's, it's about all.
And we used to go to a place called Buttes Chaumont.
Yes.
That was a-- one of the biggest park, park in, in Paris itself.
Today it still exists.
You know, that-- I used to go-- this I remember very well.
We used to go on picnics and things like that.
With the family or with friends?
With my sister and my brother.
Of course-- of course, with my parents.
Of course, yes.
And my cousins.
Yes.
Mhm.
We lived in a sort of-- in a-- in an apartment.
It was a cul-de-sac.
And up on the first floor, we lived in the apartment.
And of course, there was a concierge, like everywhere in Paris.
But I have a very, very small recollection of it.
Well, we had a-- an auntie lived upstairs on the fourth, fourth or fifth floor, which we saw during the war.
And she died after the war.
Yes.
My mother.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
She was a hardworking lady.
She used to-- she used to make a lot of clothes at home for us.
And I remember, she had a sewing machine.
In those days, I think everybody had, you know, a sewing machine.
Mhm.
Yes.
Because I saw that particular machine after the war.
But I'll tell you that in the sequence as it comes through with the--
Yes.
No, I was too small to go-- to go to school.
I vaguely recollect, not far from where we were, there was a school I used to go.
You know, like a kindergarten type of things.
My mother used to walk me every now and again.
And I can't remember exactly the street anymore.
I know-- I know where it is.
But I don't remember the street.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
And with my brother.
Mhm.
She was at school already, yes.
Yes.
In the girls' school.
Yeah.
No.
The only time I remember is that in 1939, when the war started, we'd hear the siren.
So we used to go into the cellar.
And we had gas masks, as well.
You know, and-- for fear that they were going to bomb Paris.
And it got worse and worse as time went on.
No.
No.
No, no.
But this is when Rabbi Schneersohn assembled-- you know, spoke to my parents, and Spoke to hundreds of families.
And asked them to assemble at, at the station in-- the station was Gare de Lyon-- to, to move into-- to get all the children to Vichy.
When it got-- when we heard that they invaded the north of-- north of Europe or something, you know.
The German came in and it's getting-- it was getting worse and worse, bombing and all that.
So somehow, he was able to assemble all those children-- which we were a part of it-- to a children's home.
And we ended up in Chateau Brout-Vernet-- Chateau des Morelles in Brout-Vernet in Departement Allier.
And it was-- I think it was about 40 or 60 kilometers from Vichy.
It was safe for us to be there.
And it was a, a religious-- when I say religious, we didn't eat any treif at all.
Although we didn't have too much food, anyway.
But we never ate treif there at all.
It was quite, quite strict.
Early-- late 1939, beginning 1940.
Yes.
I can remember that we walked-- we walked down to-- I have a vivid memory with this.
I was only, you know, as I said, five, six years old.
And we hailed a taxi to go to Gare de Lyon with our suitcase.
And we said goodbye to my parents.
And we thought a long journey was fun.
And we assembled with 120 boys and girls.
So we were in a train to Brout-Vernet.
And that's about the only time I remember that section.
And then, that sort of fade away, you see.
This, I couldn't remember.
I thought we were going, maybe, on a holiday or something like that.
That was my reaction.
But I didn't realize that we were going away for good, you see?
So-- and in the-- and in the home, we met friends of the family-- the children of the friends of the family.
And I must say that I still see them today.
When I go to France, we visit one another.
Yeah.
Mhm.
Well, I, I was very happy to be on the train.
And this, this-- yes, I was quite happy.
And not thinking that I would never come back.
That's about it.
And so you know, we made our life in Chateau des Morelles.
It was a chateau which was given by the baron of [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was a, a beautiful-- what you call-- a beautiful chateau, which was built in the 17th century.
It was one, one square kilometer, the ground.
So we had really a fantastic time.
You know, even though-- and every now an again, my mother used to send us a letter or something like that.
Or we had-- friends of the family used to come and visit.
Once or twice, that's about it.
And what news did you get of your parents?
That everything was all right.
And you know, my sister-- whatever my sister relayed to us.
That's it.
You know?
And until-- it was in 1942 when my sisters took us in the corner somewhere in the-- in the garden in the home.
And she told us that she was taken to, to Drancy, which was a camp, a camp.
From there, most of the people used to-- used to go to Auschwitz.
Mhm.
It was a rassemblement, you know?
The famous, the famous Vel' d'Hiver, where they, they picked up all the Jews and put them into that, that area.
You know, and so I remember that then I had tears in my eyes.
I was very young, happy.
And you know, when you're small, you don't realize, really, the-- all about it, you see?
You know?
Can you describe the house, the maison des Morelles?
Chateau des Morelles?
Yes.
Chateau des Morelles was a, a very big home.
And it looked like-- almost like Buckingham Palace.
And the corridors were-- the corridors were all wooden floors.
And because we were so many-- so we were given task to clean the floors once a week.
We used to get these-- what do you call these?
To rub the, the floor with metal brush.
And then polish, polish them again.
And if you were punished, that was-- you had to do that not only once or twice, but for one month you had to do it.
So we had to be very careful.
But it, it took-- this type of work, we didn't like-- we didn't like to do.
But the home had to be looked after.
And we had, of course, eventually, rats, as big as rabbits practically, used to-- because we left a lot of food all over the place, you know?
It was clean.
But of course, being an old chateau-- so we had all the-- we used to try and catch them.
[LAUGHS]
We were 120 boys and girls.
Well, I was six and seven by then.
And up to the age of about 16.
I was one of the youngest, yes.
I was one of the youngest.
And we used to go to school.
The school was two kilometers away.
So in France, you come back for lunch.
And you go back after lunch and so on.
And so in the morning, we used to walk in to rows of about four or five abreast.
And we used to walk down to the village to Brout-Vernet, which was two kilometers.
At lunchtime, we used to come back.
After lunch, we used to go back to, to school.
And at half past four, five o'clock, we used to come back.
So and-- in the winter time, it was absolutely terrible.
Because by the time we reach in the-- on the road, we had to carry a wooden shovel to, to get it-- to get through this-- to get to the road to go to school.
And that's one.
And I can remember that, you know, being, being a country area, our school friends-- non-Jews-- used to bring a lot of food and they used to place them on shelves.
So of course, we couldn't help ourselves but to-- well, let's say, to pinch them, you know?
Because we were starving.
And also, we used to have in the-- in this one square kilometer in our home, in the garden, we had fruit, fruit trees.
So we always used to watch them growing and growing.
Pears and apple.
And they were barely ripe that we used to pinch them.
And so on.
And I'll never forget, also, that we used to have walnuts.
So we found a step ladder.
And we walked, walked up a bit and we put the ladder against the tree.
And not knowing that downstairs, there was the farmer.
It belonged, actually, to us, but he looked after the thing.
He stood there waiting for us to come down.
We got the shock of our lives when he-- [LAUGHS]
--when he was there.
He said, OK, you two.
Carry the ladder.
And you're punished, you see?
So we walked in front.
By the time he closed some gates, you see, we just dropped the ladder and then off we took.
[LAUGHS]
So they tried to, to find us in, in the dining room, when everybody used to be-- used to sit.
And when-- walnuts, you know, when they still got the-- what do you call?
That green-- before they're ripe, All your lips becomes brown.
And your teeth.
That was it.
That's him.
That's him.
He used to pick us up like that.
And I must say, that also, it was a religious home.
So Shabbat was Shabbat with us.
We used to do the benching.
And on various-- on many occasions, I was asked to do the benching.
And you know, it, it it was really-- that's where I got my, my, my training in Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH], really.
So it was-- yeah.
Well, we-- there was a bit of anti-Semitism, definitely.
Because we often used to fight.
They used to call us-- in French, it's called, you know, dirty Jew or something like that.
And of course, we used to gang-- they ganged up against us and we'd do the same thing.
But that-- it was the norm in those days.
And we had to be very careful, because we could be denounced.
And it got-- it kept on getting worse and worse as time went by.
We saw German patrol coming on horses in that area.
And somehow, they didn't know there was a Jewish home.
Or maybe-- I, I have an idea that the French police just kept them away from us, you see?
And we were there till 1943.
How, as a little boy of six and seven, were you aware that you shouldn't be seen by Germans or--
Well, they, they kept on telling us.
And of course, you know, I think we learned that the Germans were, were-- well, when I found out that my parents were taken to a camp-- my, my father was taken-- was taken to camp, and the family.
And we heard stories of other boys and girls, of their parents.
So we realized we have to be very careful.
We were speaking Yiddish-- fluent Yiddish.
And they told us not to speak Yiddish, to continue to, to speak French and to be very careful not to speak Yiddish when we used to go on excursions.
Often, we used to go on excursions, you know?
And of course, we didn't wear yarmulkes because-- we only wore beret, which is the norm in France.
Of course, inside the dining hall, we'd wear our yarmulkes for eating.
And I remember that sometimes-- I was quite friendly with Mr.
Cogon, who used to gather the food.
He used to go from farm to farm to get milk and supplies.
And he had-- you know, he was driving to the farms to pick up the vegetables.
And also, we were assigned to pick up milk in these huge-- what do they call it?
Those huge metal thing.
And so we went-- half a dozen of us-- to pick up the milk.
And of course, we sneaked in a-- a little cup.
And by the time we got back, you see, there was quite a-- quite a bit missing.
So we decided to fill it in with water.
You see?
So-- but that was the norm.
Everybody used to do that, you know?
And-- I mean, you know, looking back, we had a good time there.
But of course, being small, we didn't realize, in many ways, what the outcome of it.
You see?
And there was a camaraderie?
There was a big-- yes.
Yes.
I can't remember having any fight-- I mean, there must have been fights, of course.
But not to the extent that hated one another.
I think that we helped one another.
That was the thing.
And I remember when I came there, originally, my mother had given me lots of shirts and things.
And I was a little bit disappointed when the teacher, who looked after us said, OK.
You have to give part of your shirts to so and so because he has none.
So we had to share everything.
In fact, we used to-- whenever you-- we used to eat an apple, a friend used to say, can you leave me the core?
You know?
I'll give you the core if you give me one of your slices of bread.
We used to trade like that.
And-- till the teacher found out.
That was forbidden to do that sort of thing.
But that was the norm amongst ourselves, you see?
You know?
Hm.
And the teachers that were looking after you-- are you talking about teachers at school?
No, no, no.
The teachers at home, you see?
Being 120 boys and girls-- if I can remember, there was one director who was in charge, the headmaster.
And there were-- we were-- we had so many groups.
And I was assigned to a-- to a group.
And it was run by Mr. and-- Monsieur et Madame Dybnis.
Dybnis.
D-Y-B-N-I-S.
Dybnis.
And Miriam-- that was her first name-- and Henri.
In fact, when Henri Dybnis came to our home, they got married.
The two got married.
It was a big wedding there.
Well, not a big wedding.
To us, it was a big wedding.
And so they were like-- virtually like parents to us.
You see?
That was my group with them.
And-- How many in your group?
In our group, we must have been about 20, 30 people. 30 boys and girls.
And so what-- because you see, not all of us could go, let's say, on the excursion.
[COUGHS] Excuse me.
Because we were too many.
Excuse me.
[DRINKS]
So we had to make smaller group, of course.
And we had to be careful.
Because outside the home in the country, every now and again we would see a German patrol coming.
Somehow, they were always on horses or walking, the German patrol, you see?
So by the 1943, it got very-- it became very bad.
In September.
I'm sorry.
Yeah.
I came in 19-- end of '39, '40.
Yeah.
Yes.
We stayed in Brout-Vernet right up to 19-- April, 1943.
Somehow, we were notified-- the, the headmaster was notified that the Germans were coming to pick us up the following day.
Please get out quickly.
And so we left everything.
And we split into various groups.
My group was still with, with the Dybnis.
And we went to-- we went to Grenoble, up in the mountains, au Chateau des Manoir.
Chateau des Manoir was run by Rabbi Schneersohn.
Yeah.
Chateau des Morelles, yes.
Yeah.
We always-- we never split.
My brother and I, we always got-- we were always together.
Yes.
Yes.
And she was-- my sister was virtually like, like a mother to me.
Because if I-- if I did the wrong thing, that -- I'd get the back of her hand.
So I had to be very-- she was very strict with us.
Looking back, it was a good idea, maybe, you know?
But--
